_meta.purpose: Per-state roof data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages
_meta.sources: IECC 2021 climate zone map; NOAA SPC hail climatology; ASCE 7-22 wind zones; state contractor license boards (verified URLs); BLS OEWS May 2024 (roofer SOC 47-2181 state means).

fields.iecc_zone: 2021 IECC climate zone (1-8, A=moist, B=dry, C=marine)
fields.hurricane_tier: HVHZ (FL Miami-Dade/Broward only) / wind_high (140mph+ design) / wind_moderate (110-140) / wind_low (<110) / none
fields.hail_tier: very_high / high / moderate / low (NOAA SPC severe hail days)
fields.snow_load_psf: ASCE 7-22 ground snow load typical of state's main population zone (psf)
fields.license_status: required (license board enforces) / registration (registration only, no exam) / none (state has no statewide roofing license)
fields.permit_typical: Common municipal permit-required threshold for re-roof
fields.roofer_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 47-2181 Roofers (USD)
fields.avg_replacement_low: Typical low end of 2,000 sqft asphalt replacement (USD)
fields.avg_replacement_high: Typical high end (premium material or steep/complex) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific roofing/insurance/contractor rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate driver of roof system selection in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.license_board_name: Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board (residential roofing $10K+)
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Code §27-12A-20 prohibits roofers from offering to pay or rebate insurance deductibles — a violation is a misdemeanor.
AL.climate_concern: Tropical-system-driven wind uplift along the Gulf Coast, with Mobile and Baldwin counties on a 140-mph wind-design line. Inland storms drop heavy rain that pushes ridge and valley flashing failures.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Construction Contractors
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska Statute 08.18 requires contractors to carry a $20,000 surety bond and proof of workers' compensation before any residential roof job, regardless of size.
AK.climate_concern: Ground snow load of 50–300 psf depending on borough drives standing-seam metal as the dominant choice; ice-dam mitigation (heated edges, full ice-and-water shield) is standard, not optional.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license C-42 (roofing)
AZ.permit_typical: city or county permit required for re-roof
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona ROC license C-42 is mandatory for any roofing work above $1,000 — unlicensed contracting is a Class 1 misdemeanor and voids any contract claim.
AZ.climate_concern: Sustained high UV and surface temperatures above 160°F on dark-colored shingles drive concrete and clay tile dominance in the Phoenix and Tucson basins; flat-roof foam is common on mid-century homes.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board — residential remodeler license required for jobs over $2,000
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas Act 1027 (2017) prohibits roofers from waiving or paying any portion of an insurance deductible, with a $1,000 fine and potential license revocation per offense.
AR.climate_concern: Arkansas sits inside the central U.S. hail corridor — Class 4 impact-resistant shingles qualify for state-mandated insurance discounts under Bulletin 2-2014 from the Insurance Department.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-39 (Roofing) classification
CA.permit_typical: permit and Title 24 cool-roof compliance required
CA.distinctive_law: California Title 24 Part 6 mandates cool-roof minimum solar reflectance and thermal emittance values — non-compliant materials cannot be installed in low-slope re-roofs in most climate zones.
CA.climate_concern: Wildland-urban-interface zones in CAL FIRE high-severity hazard areas require Class A fire-rated assemblies under Chapter 7A of the California Building Code; asphalt with code-listed underlayment or non-combustible coverings (tile, metal) are standard.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — most municipalities (Denver, Colorado Springs, Aurora) require local registration
CO.permit_typical: city permit required, separate by municipality
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado Senate Bill 38 (2012) prohibits roofers from paying, waiving, or rebating any deductible and requires a 72-hour right of rescission on storm-damage roof contracts; HB18-1342 added separate hail-deductible disclosure requirements.
CO.climate_concern: The Front Range from Fort Collins through Pueblo sits in the highest-frequency hail belt in the country; impact-resistant Class 4 shingles are heavily incentivized by state-domiciled insurers (USAA, State Farm) and frequently mandated for renewal in Larimer and El Paso counties.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor registration
CT.permit_typical: town building permit required for full re-roof
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut Home Improvement Act §20-429 voids any unwritten roofing contract over $200 and bars unregistered contractors from collecting any payment, including in court.
CT.climate_concern: Coastal Fairfield and New London counties carry a 130-mph design wind under ASCE 7-22; ice-dam protection (minimum 24 inches inside the heated wall line) is a code requirement on every eave and valley.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Revenue — business license; no separate statewide roofing license
DE.permit_typical: county permit required (Sussex, Kent, New Castle)
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware Code Title 6 §2533 requires written contracts and a three-day cooling-off period for any home improvement work over $500 — unsigned change orders are unenforceable.
DE.climate_concern: Sussex County beach communities (Rehoboth, Lewes, Bethany) sit on the 130-mph wind line and require enhanced fastening schedules; salt-air corrosion shortens galvanized fastener life on ocean-facing slopes.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida CILB — Certified Roofing Contractor (CCC) for statewide; Registered Roofing Contractor (RC) for local
FL.permit_typical: permit required statewide; HVHZ has separate product approval
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Statute §489.147 (anti-AOB law) and the 25% Rule under §708.1.1.1 of the Florida Building Code interact: if more than 25% of a roof in a 12-month period is damaged or repaired, the entire roof must be brought up to current code — a defining cost driver in Miami-Dade and Broward HVHZ jurisdictions where Notice of Acceptance (NOA) product approval is mandatory.
FL.climate_concern: Miami-Dade and Broward counties form the High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ) with 175-mph design winds, mandatory product approval (NOA), and secondary water barrier requirements — coastal Panhandle counties carry 150-mph design loads.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — general contractor license required for jobs over $2,500
GA.distinctive_law: Georgia OCGA §43-41-17 exempts roofing-only work from the general contractor licensing requirement, but jobs bundled with structural or framing work (sheathing replacement above 50% of deck) trigger the full license — a frequent gray area on storm jobs.
GA.climate_concern: Atlanta metro experiences a higher pollen-and-debris load than nearly any U.S. metro, accelerating organic-shingle algae streaking; algae-resistant (AR-rated) shingles are now de facto standard across the metro.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii's Contractor Recovery Fund (HRS §444-26) caps homeowner restitution from licensed-contractor disputes at $25,000 per claimant — and only licensed C-42b roofers are eligible, making license verification a homeowner-side risk control.
HI.climate_concern: Trade-wind exposure on windward (north and east) Oahu and Hawaii Island slopes drives a 130–140 mph design wind; saltwater corrosion on coastal lots requires stainless or hot-dip galvanized fasteners and shortens nominal life of standard-zinc components.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Contractors Board — registration only, no exam
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho Code §54-5208 requires every roofer to be registered with a $4,000 cash bond or surety equivalent — no exam, but proof of liability and workers' compensation insurance must be filed before work begins.
ID.climate_concern: Sun Valley, McCall, and Bonner County mountain communities carry ground snow loads above 80 psf; engineered ridge ventilation and step-flashing at log-home profiles are the dominant cost drivers in upper-elevation work.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.license_board_name: Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation — Roofing Contractor license
IL.distinctive_law: Illinois Roofing Industry Licensing Act (225 ILCS 335) requires every roofing company to hold a single state license tied to a qualifying party — the highest hourly roofer wage in the country (BLS OEWS) reflects the strong union (Local 11) presence in Chicago metro.
IL.climate_concern: The freeze-thaw cycle through Cook, DuPage, and Lake counties (over 70 transitions per winter) drives ice-dam formation and demands continuous ice-and-water shield from eave to 24 inches inside the heated wall, a code-mandatory item under the IRC adoption.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — most municipalities require local registration
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana Code §24-5-11 (Home Improvement Contracts Act) voids any contract over $150 that lacks specific written terms — and gives consumers a three-day cancellation window on door-to-door storm-chaser pitches.
IN.climate_concern: Tornado-belt activity in the southern half of the state (Evansville, Bloomington, Indianapolis south) drives demand for sealed-edge fastening patterns and 6-nail high-wind shingle warranties, which are upcharges on standard installations.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Division of Labor — Contractor Registration ($2,000 fee + bond)
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Code §103A.71 (effective 2014) prohibits roofers from offering to pay or rebate insurance deductibles and requires a 90-day insurance-claim window disclosure on storm-damage contracts.
IA.climate_concern: The 2020 derecho exposed weaknesses in standard 4-nail shingle patterns; six-nail high-wind installations and synthetic underlayment are now the de facto standard in Linn, Polk, and Scott counties.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.license_board_name: Kansas Roofing Registration (KSA 44-153) — annual registration, $300 fee
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas was the first state (2013) to require statewide roofer registration with the Attorney General — KSA 44-153 imposes a $25,000 surety bond and bars any roofer from rebating insurance deductibles, with criminal penalties for repeat offenses.
KS.climate_concern: The Wichita-to-Kansas City corridor sees 8–12 severe hail days per year on average; insurance carriers (Shelter, American Family) discount Class 4 impact-resistant shingles 15–30% on premium.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — Louisville Metro and Lexington-Fayette require local registration
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky's Home Solicitation Sales Act (KRS 367.410) provides a three-day right of rescission for roofing contracts signed at the homeowner's residence, and KRS 304.12-095 prohibits insurance-deductible rebating.
KY.climate_concern: The lower Ohio River valley produces frequent wind-and-hail squall lines from March through July; western counties (Henderson, Daviess) sit in the Tornado Alley fringe with 130-mph design wind requirements on new construction.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors — Residential Roofing classification
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana Revised Statute §22:1264 enacted post-Katrina caps the deductible-rebating prohibition with $5,000 fines per violation; the Louisiana Citizens Property Insurance Corporation provides last-resort hurricane coverage for coastal homeowners denied private market policies.
LA.climate_concern: ASCE 7-22 wind speeds along Cameron, Plaquemines, and St. Bernard parishes reach 150–160 mph for design wind — Miami-Dade-style enhanced fastening (6 nails, 1.25-inch ring shank) and starter-strip drip-edge are standard.

ME.name: Maine
ME.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — registration required for general home improvement contractors over $1,400
ME.distinctive_law: Maine's Home Construction Contracts Act (10 MRSA §1486-1490) requires written contracts on any home job over $3,000 — and limits down-payments to one-third of the contract value.
ME.climate_concern: Northern Maine winters drive ground snow loads of 70–100 psf; full ice-and-water-shield underlayment from eave to ridge on low-slope sections (4/12 and below) is a routine spec, and standing-seam metal dominates lake and coastal vacation builds.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.license_board_name: Maryland Home Improvement Commission — MHIC license required for any roofing job over $500
MD.permit_typical: county permit (Montgomery, Prince George's, Baltimore, Anne Arundel)
MD.distinctive_law: Maryland's Guaranty Fund (MD Code Bus. Reg. §8-405) reimburses homeowners up to $20,000 per licensed-contractor dispute — verifying MHIC license status before signing is the highest-leverage homeowner-side check in the state.
MD.climate_concern: Eastern Shore (Worcester, Wicomico, Somerset) sits inside the Atlantic hurricane envelope with 130-mph design wind; Western Maryland (Garrett, Allegany) carries a 50-psf snow load and routine ice-dam exposure.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.license_board_name: Massachusetts Office of Consumer Affairs — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration; CSL (Construction Supervisor License) for structural work
MA.distinctive_law: Massachusetts has the country's most consumer-protective home-improvement framework: the Guaranty Fund (M.G.L. c.142A §5) reimburses up to $10,000 per claim against registered contractors, and the Attorney General's office actively prosecutes unregistered roofers under Chapter 93A.
MA.climate_concern: Cape Cod, Nantucket, and Martha's Vineyard sit on the 140-mph wind-design line and require enhanced fastening (six nails, ring-shank) and storm-rated starter strips; salt exposure shortens galvanized fastener life on ocean-facing slopes.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.license_board_name: Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs — Residential Builder license required for jobs over $600
MI.distinctive_law: Michigan's Residential Builders Act (Public Act 299 of 1980) makes it a misdemeanor to roof for compensation without a license on jobs above $600 — and bars unlicensed contractors from collecting in court even for completed work.
MI.climate_concern: Upper Peninsula (Marquette, Houghton) carries ground snow loads of 70–90 psf and lake-effect ice-dam frequency unmatched in the Lower 48; Lower Peninsula freeze-thaw counts top 60 cycles per winter, making valley flashing and ice-and-water shield selection a long-term cost driver.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.license_board_name: Minnesota Department of Labor and Industry — Residential Roofer license (Minn. Stat. §326B.802)
MN.distinctive_law: Minnesota Statute §325E.66 (the Roofing Contractor Statute) prohibits insurance-deductible rebating, requires written contracts with itemized scope, and mandates a five-day right of rescission on storm-damage contracts — among the most aggressive consumer-protection roofing laws in the country.
MN.climate_concern: Twin Cities and Duluth winters combine 35-psf snow loads with hail-belt exposure (high frequency in May–August); Class 4 impact-resistant shingles plus full ice-and-water shield are the de facto standard in Hennepin, Ramsey, and St. Louis counties.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.license_board_name: Mississippi State Board of Contractors — Residential Roofing classification (jobs over $50,000); Residential Builders Board for general residential contracts
MS.distinctive_law: Mississippi Code §83-15-79 prohibits roofers from offering to absorb or rebate insurance deductibles on storm claims — a misdemeanor with up to a $1,000 fine per offense and license revocation.
MS.climate_concern: Coastal Hancock, Harrison, and Jackson counties carry 160-mph design winds under ASCE 7-22 and post-Katrina building-code amendments — secondary water barriers and ring-shank fastening are state-mandatory in Wind Zone IV.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — Kansas City, St. Louis, and Springfield require municipal registration
MO.distinctive_law: Missouri Senate Bill 564 (2014) prohibits roofers from rebating insurance deductibles on storm claims and requires the contract to include explicit insurance-claim language; SB 631 (2018) added a mandatory 5-day right of rescission.
MO.climate_concern: The Kansas City and St. Louis corridors sit inside the central U.S. hail belt — a Class 4 impact-resistant shingle qualifies for an automatic 10–25% premium discount under Missouri Department of Insurance Bulletin 2014-08.

MT.name: Montana
MT.license_board_name: Montana Department of Labor and Industry — Independent Contractor or Construction Contractor registration
MT.distinctive_law: Montana Code §39-9 mandates a $5,000 minimum surety bond for independent contractors and proof of workers' compensation before any registered roofer can pull permits — unregistered work is unenforceable in court under §28-2-101.
MT.climate_concern: Mountain communities (Bozeman, Whitefish, Big Sky) carry ground snow loads of 70–120 psf; engineered ice-dam protection and steep-pitch (8/12+) snow shedding are dominant cost drivers in resort markets.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.license_board_name: Nebraska Department of Labor — Contractor Registration ($25 fee, no exam)
NE.distinctive_law: Nebraska's Roofing Contractor Insurance Disclosure Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. §44-8601) prohibits insurance-deductible rebating and requires roofers to provide an itemized scope before starting any storm-damage repair.
NE.climate_concern: The Omaha–Lincoln corridor is one of the highest-frequency hail markets in the U.S.; impact-resistant Class 4 shingles qualify for state-approved insurance discounts under Nebraska Department of Insurance Bulletin CB-127.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.license_board_name: Nevada State Contractors Board — C-15a (Roofing) classification
NV.permit_typical: Clark or Washoe County permit required
NV.distinctive_law: Nevada Revised Statutes §624.020 prohibits any roofing work over $1,000 without a C-15a license — and the Recovery Fund (NRS §624.470) provides up to $40,000 per claim against a licensed contractor's defective work.
NV.climate_concern: Las Vegas and Reno see surface temperatures above 160°F on dark roofs through summer; concrete and clay-tile dominance plus white-cap modified bitumen on flat roofs are the climate-driven norms.

NH.name: New Hampshire
NH.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license; general home improvement consumer protection under NH RSA 358-A
NH.distinctive_law: New Hampshire's Consumer Protection Act (RSA 358-A) provides triple damages for unfair or deceptive home-improvement practices — and applies to deductible rebating, even though there is no separate roofing licensing statute.
NH.climate_concern: Northern New Hampshire (Coos, Carroll, Grafton counties) carries ground snow loads of 60–100 psf; ice-dam protection and 30-degree (or steeper) pitch are the dominant cost drivers, and standing-seam metal is heavily used in mountain communities.

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.license_board_name: New Jersey Division of Consumer Affairs — Home Improvement Contractor registration (HIC) required
NJ.distinctive_law: New Jersey's Contractors' Registration Act (N.J.S.A. 56:8-136) requires HIC registration on any roofing job over $500 and bars unregistered contractors from collecting in court — and mandates a three-day right of rescission on door-to-door sales.
NJ.climate_concern: Atlantic, Cape May, and Ocean counties sit on the 130-mph wind-design line; salt-air corrosion shortens galvanized-fastener life on coastal slopes, and ring-shank stainless-steel fasteners are common upgrades in Long Beach Island and Cape May markets.

NM.name: New Mexico
NM.license_board_name: New Mexico Construction Industries Division — GB-2 Residential Roofing classification
NM.distinctive_law: New Mexico's Construction Industries Licensing Act (NMSA §60-13) requires a state license for any roofing job over $7,200 and exposes unlicensed contractors to civil penalties up to $10,000 per violation.
NM.climate_concern: Adobe, pueblo, and territorial homes use parapet-walled flat or low-slope roofs covered in built-up bitumen or single-ply membrane — replacing or restoring these systems differs sharply in scope from a typical pitched-roof asphalt project elsewhere in the state.

NY.name: New York
NY.license_board_name: Local: NYC DCWP Home Improvement Contractor; Westchester, Suffolk, Nassau, Rockland — county HIC license; no single statewide license
NY.distinctive_law: New York General Business Law §771 voids any home-improvement contract over $500 that lacks the required written terms — and Article 36-A allows escrow-account requirements for any down-payment over 30% of contract value.
NY.climate_concern: Long Island carries 130-mph design winds and salt exposure; the Adirondacks and Tug Hill plateau (Lewis, Oswego counties) carry some of the highest ground snow loads (70–100 psf) east of the Rockies, and ice-dam mitigation is a year-round cost driver.

NC.name: North Carolina
NC.license_board_name: North Carolina Licensing Board for General Contractors — license required for jobs over $30,000
NC.distinctive_law: North Carolina General Statute §87-1 sets the unlicensed-contracting threshold at $30,000 — below that, any tradesperson can roof; above it, only a NCLBGC-licensed general contractor can pull permits.
NC.climate_concern: Outer Banks and coastal Carteret, Brunswick, New Hanover counties carry 130–140 mph design winds; the eastern half of the state experiences regular tropical-storm-driven uplift and post-storm wind-pattern damage — six-nail high-wind installation is the regional standard.

ND.name: North Dakota
ND.license_board_name: North Dakota Secretary of State — Contractor License (NDCC 43-07) required for jobs over $4,000
ND.distinctive_law: North Dakota Century Code §43-07 imposes a graduated license-fee schedule based on contract size; any roofer working a job over $500,000 must hold a Class A license with a $25,000 bond.
ND.climate_concern: Combined hail exposure (Fargo, Bismarck) and severe ground snow loads (35 psf+ across the Red River Valley) make impact-resistant Class 4 shingles plus full ice-and-water shield the standard — and the dominant cost-vs-baseline upgrade in the state.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.license_board_name: No statewide residential roofing license — Cleveland, Cincinnati, Columbus require municipal registration; commercial roofing requires Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board
OH.distinctive_law: Ohio Revised Code §4722 (Home Construction Service Suppliers Act) requires written contracts on any home job over $25,000 and gives consumers a 3-day right of rescission on door-to-door storm-chaser pitches, with treble damages for violations.
OH.climate_concern: Lake-effect ice damming on northern Ohio counties (Cuyahoga, Lake, Ashtabula) drives full ice-and-water-shield specs from eave to 24 inches inside the heated wall; freeze-thaw counts (60+/winter) put valley flashing and step-flashing at the top of the long-term cost-driver list.

OK.name: Oklahoma
OK.license_board_name: Oklahoma Construction Industries Board — Roofing Contractor Registration (Title 59 §1151.21)
OK.distinctive_law: Oklahoma's Roofing Contractor Registration Act (Title 59 §1151.21–1151.31) mandates statewide registration with a $1,000 surety bond and prohibits insurance-deductible rebating — the Oklahoma Insurance Department actively enforces post-storm.
OK.climate_concern: Oklahoma City and Tulsa metros are at the heart of Tornado Alley and Hail Alley overlap — Class 4 impact-resistant shingles plus six-nail high-wind installation patterns are the dominant insurance-eligible upgrade combination.

OR.name: Oregon
OR.license_board_name: Oregon Construction Contractors Board — license required for any roofing work for compensation
OR.distinctive_law: Oregon Revised Statute §701 mandates CCB licensing on any structure-altering work — even unpaid roofing for a friend triggers liability if unlicensed; the CCB Recovery Fund covers up to $20,000 per residential consumer claim against a licensed contractor.
OR.climate_concern: The Willamette Valley's persistent winter rain and moss growth drive zinc and copper-strip ridge installations and frequent gentle pressure-washing schedules; coastal Lincoln, Tillamook, Clatsop counties see saltwater corrosion and 110-mph Pacific-storm wind exposure.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.license_board_name: Pennsylvania Office of Attorney General — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration required for jobs over $5,000 annually
PA.distinctive_law: Pennsylvania's Home Improvement Consumer Protection Act (HICPA, Act 132 of 2008) requires written contracts on jobs over $500, caps deposits at one-third of contract value, and bars deductible rebating with treble damages on consumer claims.
PA.climate_concern: The Pocono Mountains and Erie lake-effect belt produce 70–80-psf snow loads in Wayne, Pike, and Erie counties; full ice-and-water-shield underlayment and 8/12+ pitch shedding are dominant in the upper-elevation markets.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.license_board_name: Rhode Island Contractors' Registration and Licensing Board — registration required for any roofing work
RI.distinctive_law: Rhode Island General Laws §5-65 mandates registration on every home-improvement contractor, including roofers — unregistered roofers cannot pull permits or collect on contracts, and the CRB enforces with up to $5,000 fines per violation.
RI.climate_concern: Newport, Block Island, and South County coastal exposures sit on the 140-mph wind-design line and require enhanced fastening; the state's small geography means saltwater-air corrosion affects nearly all roofs within 5 miles of the shoreline.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.license_board_name: South Carolina LLR Residential Builders Commission — Specialty Contractor (Roofing)
SC.distinctive_law: South Carolina Code §40-59 requires a residential specialty-contractor license on any roofing job over $5,000 — and the South Carolina Department of Insurance Bulletin 2014-06 prohibits deductible rebating on storm claims.
SC.climate_concern: Charleston, Beaufort, Horry, and Georgetown counties carry 140-mph design winds under ASCE 7-22 and post-Hugo amendments; secondary water barrier and ring-shank fastener requirements are common in coastal Wind Zone IV jurisdictions.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.license_board_name: No statewide license — Sioux Falls and Rapid City require municipal registration
SD.distinctive_law: South Dakota Codified Laws §37-24 (Deceptive Trade Practices) gives consumers a private right of action with treble damages against any roofer who misrepresents storm damage or attempts deductible rebating.
SD.climate_concern: The Black Hills region (Rapid City, Spearfish) carries elevated ground snow loads (40–60 psf) and frequent severe hail; eastern South Dakota (Sioux Falls) sits firmly inside the central hail corridor with Class 4 shingle adoption growing.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.license_board_name: Tennessee Board for Licensing Contractors — required for residential jobs over $25,000
TN.distinctive_law: Tennessee Code §62-6 imposes the country's strictest unlicensed-contracting penalty: any roofer working without a license on a job over $25,000 commits a Class A misdemeanor on the first offense and a Class E felony on the second.
TN.climate_concern: Middle and West Tennessee (Nashville, Memphis, Jackson) experience seasonal severe-thunderstorm wind events and quasi-tornadic squall lines from March through May; six-nail high-wind shingle installations are increasingly specified by insurers in Davidson, Shelby, and Madison counties.

TX.name: Texas
TX.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license — no exam, no bond requirement; some cities (Houston, Dallas, Austin) require local registration
TX.distinctive_law: Texas Insurance Code §707 (the Roofing Insurance Anti-Rebating Statute) prohibits roofers from waiving or rebating insurance deductibles on residential storm claims; Texas Department of Insurance Order 2013-1 caps a contractor's cosmetic-versus-functional damage assessment exposure on hail claims.
TX.climate_concern: Texas spans hurricane wind zones (Houston, Galveston, Corpus Christi 140-mph) and the heart of Hail Alley (Dallas–Fort Worth, San Antonio, Austin) — Class 4 impact-resistant shingles plus six-nail high-wind installation are the dominant combined insurance-driven upgrade.

UT.name: Utah
UT.license_board_name: Utah Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing — R200 Roofing Contractor
UT.distinctive_law: Utah Code §58-55 requires the R200 specialty roofer license for any roofing work for compensation, and §58-55-308 makes unlicensed roofing a Class B misdemeanor — repeat offenses trigger civil penalties up to $5,000 per violation.
UT.climate_concern: The Wasatch Front winter inversion and lake-effect snow drive ground snow loads of 30–50 psf in Salt Lake, Davis, and Weber counties; the Wasatch Back resort communities (Park City, Heber) routinely require 70+ psf snow-load engineering.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.license_board_name: Vermont Office of Professional Regulation — Residential Contractor Registration (effective 2021)
VT.distinctive_law: Vermont Act 182 of 2020 (effective 2021) created the country's most recent statewide residential contractor registration scheme, applying to any home-improvement work for compensation including roofing.
VT.climate_concern: Northern Vermont (Caledonia, Essex, Orleans counties) and the Green Mountain spine carry ground snow loads of 60–90 psf; standing-seam metal is widely used in mountain communities, and ice-dam protection is a permanent year-round consideration.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.license_board_name: Virginia DPOR Board for Contractors — Class C (jobs $0–$10K), Class B ($10K–$120K), Class A (over $120K)
VA.distinctive_law: Virginia Code §54.1-1100 imposes graduated contractor licensing tied to job value — Class C roofers can take residential work up to $10,000, Class B up to $120,000, Class A above; unlicensed work over $1,000 is a Class 1 misdemeanor under §54.1-111.
VA.climate_concern: Coastal Hampton Roads (Virginia Beach, Norfolk, Chesapeake) carries 130-mph design winds; the Blue Ridge and Allegheny corridors (Augusta, Highland, Bath counties) carry 30–50 psf snow loads and freeze-thaw exposure that drives valley flashing and ice-shield specs.

WA.name: Washington
WA.license_board_name: Washington Department of Labor and Industries — Contractor Registration (general or specialty)
WA.distinctive_law: Washington Revised Code §18.27 mandates contractor registration with a $12,000 bond (general) or $6,000 (specialty) and proof of liability/workers' compensation; unregistered roofers cannot file mechanic's liens or collect in court.
WA.climate_concern: The Puget Sound region's persistent moss growth and winter rain drive zinc-strip ridge installations and frequent gutter-debris management; Cascade and Olympic mountain communities (Stevens Pass, Snoqualmie) carry 60–90 psf snow loads.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.license_board_name: West Virginia Division of Labor — Contractor Licensing Board
WV.distinctive_law: West Virginia Code §21-11 requires Contractor Licensing Board registration with a $5,000 surety bond and prohibits unlicensed roofing work for compensation, with civil penalties up to $200 per day of violation.
WV.climate_concern: Mountainous terrain across the Allegheny Plateau drives 30–50 psf snow loads and steep-pitch (8/12–12/12) framing on a high share of the housing stock; freeze-thaw cycles and ridge-line wind exposure shape long-term cost-driver patterns.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.license_board_name: Wisconsin Department of Safety and Professional Services — Dwelling Contractor and Dwelling Contractor Qualifier credentials
WI.distinctive_law: Wisconsin SPS 305.31 requires every home-improvement business to hold a Dwelling Contractor credential, and SPS 305.61 mandates a Qualifier (passed exam) on staff — stricter than most Midwest states.
WI.climate_concern: Northern Wisconsin (Bayfield, Iron, Vilas counties) and the Door County peninsula carry 50–70 psf snow loads with lake-effect ice-dam frequency; full ice-and-water shield from eave to interior wall plus ridge-vent design are the dominant cost drivers.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.license_board_name: No statewide roofing license; municipal registration in Casper and Cheyenne
WY.distinctive_law: Wyoming has no statewide contractor licensing — consumer protection runs through Wyoming Statute §40-12 (Consumer Protection Act), which provides treble damages for deceptive home-improvement practices including deductible rebating.
WY.climate_concern: Mountain communities (Jackson, Pinedale, Sheridan) carry ground snow loads of 50–90 psf and sustained ridge-line wind exposure above 110 mph in exposed sites; standing-seam metal is the dominant high-elevation choice and heavier engineering is routine.

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.license_board_name: DC Department of Consumer and Regulatory Affairs — Home Improvement Contractor license
DC.distinctive_law: DC Code §47-2851 requires HIC license with a $25,000 surety bond — and the Department of Consumer and Regulatory Affairs maintains a public license-lookup tool that homeowners can verify before signing.
DC.climate_concern: DC's high share of older row houses (pre-WWII flat or low-slope) drives a heavy concentration of built-up bitumen and EPDM single-ply membrane work — system selection differs substantially from the suburban pitched-asphalt norm in surrounding Maryland and Virginia.
